items.correct: By the word's grammatical function in a sentence — not its meaning. The same word can belong to different classes depending on use (e.g. 'run' as noun or verb).
items.misconception: A noun is always a person, place or thing.
items.random_wrong: A noun is any word that starts with a capital letter.
items.why: 'Running' can be a noun ('Running is healthy'), a verb ('I am running'), or an adjective ('running water'). The notional definition fails — function in context determines word class.

items.correct: A group of words from a similar area of meaning — e.g. 'whisper, scream, mumble, shout' all belong to the semantic field of vocalisation.
items.misconception: A semantic field is a list of synonyms for one word.
items.random_wrong: A semantic field is a field of study like physics or biology.
items.why: Synonyms share meaning closely; semantic fields are broader, including related but distinct words. 'Hot' and 'cold' belong to the same semantic field (temperature) but aren't synonyms.

items.q: What is the IPA in linguistics?
items.correct: The International Phonetic Alphabet — a standardised set of symbols where each symbol represents one distinct sound. Used to transcribe pronunciation precisely across all languages.
items.misconception: The IPA is just another alphabet like the Roman or Greek alphabet.
items.random_wrong: The IPA stands for International Phonetic Acronym.
items.why: Ordinary alphabets have ambiguous symbol-sound mapping (English 'ough' has multiple pronunciations). The IPA exists precisely because regular alphabets are insufficient — one symbol = one sound, universally.

items.q: Which device is 'I told you a million times'?
items.why: It's Hyperbole. “Anaphora” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many items make a 'triplet' / rule of three?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
